attributes.stem: A 52-year-old transgender woman presents to the General Practice clinic to discuss gender-affirming care. She is commencing oestrogen therapy today. She has no significant medical history and takes no other regular medications. She has no family history of breast cancer. She asks about breast cancer screening. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate breast cancer screening recommendation?
options: No screening required as risk is minimal
attributes.explanation:
Breast cancer screening recommendations for transgender individuals should be based on individual risk factors, anatomy, and duration of hormone therapy exposure. For transgender women, the duration of oestrogen therapy is a key factor in determining breast cancer risk and screening recommendations.

Biennial mammogram starting in 5 years is the most appropriate recommendation. Current guidelines recommend that transgender women who have been on gender-affirming hormone therapy for five or more years should follow similar screening guidelines to cisgender women. Since this patient is just starting hormone therapy today, she should begin screening after 5 years of treatment, which would be at age 57, and then continue with biennial screening until age 74.

Annual mammogram starting immediately is not indicated as the patient has not yet developed sufficient breast tissue from hormone therapy, and there is no evidence to support immediate screening at the commencement of hormone therapy or the need for an reduced screening interval.

No screening required is incorrect as transgender women on long-term hormone therapy do develop breast tissue and have a measurable risk of breast cancer, albeit lower than cisgender women.

Biennial mammogram from age 50-74 would be appropriate for someone who had already been on hormone therapy for 5 years or more, but this patient is only starting therapy today.

Annual clinical breast examination only would be insufficient as a screening strategy. Clinical examination should complement, not replace, mammographic screening once the patient meets criteria for screening.

It's important to note that transgender men who have not had chest masculinisation surgery should follow the same screening guidelines as cisgender women. After chest masculinisation surgery, screening recommendations depend on the amount of remaining breast tissue and should be individualised.
learningPoints: Transgender women should begin breast cancer screening 5 years after commencing gender-affirming hormone therapy, following standard population screening guidelines thereafter.
Transgender men who have not had chest surgery should follow the same breast cancer screening guidelines as cisgender women.
After chest masculinisation surgery, screening recommendations depend on the amount of remaining breast tissue.
references: Blok CJM de, Wiepjes CM, Nota NM, Engelen K van, Adank MA, Dreijerink KMA, et al. Breast cancer risk in transgender people receiving hormone treatment: nationwide cohort study in the Netherlands. BMJ [Internet]. 2019 May 14;365. Available from: https://www.bmj.com/content/365/bmj.l1652

Information for trans and gender diverse people [Internet]. Breast Screen. 2024 [cited 2024 Nov 10]. Available from: https://www.breastscreen.nsw.gov.au/community-support/trans-and-gender-diverse-people/
